metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `reconcile_verdict` is the right confinement probe for AC2, or broaden the entry point to a caller that actually traverses the close/verify workflow path (e.g. the workflow gate op or `verify_completion` with a canned runner), so the spy test can catch realistic future regressions.
findings.finding: The confinement spy test (AC2) targets `reconcile_verdict` as its close-path entry point, but `reconcile_verdict` is a ~25-line deterministic string-normalization function that contains no import of `plan_review` and zero plausible code path to the floor — the test will trivially pass even with a broken spy setup, and it cannot catch a future regression where the floor is erroneously wired into a *different* close-path caller (e.g., the workflow gate op that calls `verify_completion`, or a future `reconcile_verdict` refactor). The plan provides no positive rationale for why `reconcile_verdict` specifically is the right confinement probe.
findings.location: What — AC2 / 'run the deterministic close-path verdict handler `rebar.llm.completion.reconcile_verdict(...)`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the confinement test's entry point: the plan must either (a) exercise the full close-path entry point that could plausibly be wired to the floor (e.g. `verify_completion` with a mocked runner, or the gate_ops path), not just `reconcile_verdict` alone, OR (b) explicitly acknowledge that `reconcile_verdict` is being chosen because it is the sole chokepoint and explain why no other close-path code path can reach the floor.
findings.finding: The confinement spy test (What §2 / AC2) installs monkeypatch spies on `plan_review._maybe_apply_completion_floor` and `plan_review._apply_completion_floor_to_verdict`, then calls `completion.reconcile_verdict`. But `reconcile_verdict` is a ~30-line deterministic function (confirmed in src/rebar/llm/completion.py:202-239) that only reads/writes `result["verdict"]`, `result["findings"]`, and `result["remediation"]` — it has no static or dynamic call path to anything in `plan_review`. The spies will NEVER fire regardless of implementation, making the test structurally vacuous. The plan's own rationale ('robust to renames/indirect calls — the spy is on the actual floor binding') is contradicted by the fact that the call graph between reconcile_verdict and plan_review.* is empty. This is strictly weaker than the old getsource check on the whole `rebar.llm.completion` module, which would have caught a call added to `verify_completion`, `deterministic_child_failure`, or gate_ops.
findings.location: What §2 / AC2: 'A behavioral test spies both floor entry points and runs completion.reconcile_verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'run `completion.reconcile_verdict(...)`' with a call to a broader close-path entry point (e.g. `gate_ops.reconcile_agent_verdict(...)` or `completion.verify_completion(...)` with a mocked runner) so the spy is on a code path that *could* reach the floor. Alternatively, if `reconcile_verdict` is intentionally minimal, update the test to also spy-guard `verify_completion` by monkeypatching `rebar.llm.completion.plan_review` (if any import exists) to demonstrate the full module boundary is clean.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4 to name exactly where the mutation is injected (e.g. 'inside `reconcile_verdict`'s body') and acknowledge that the test only guards that specific locus, OR expand the tested entry point to cover the full close-path so the mutation check is representative.
findings.finding: AC4's mutation check ('temporarily add the call; confirm RED') does not specify WHERE the mutation is injected, creating an ambiguous validation protocol. Given the spy is scoped to `reconcile_verdict`'s execution frame, only a mutation inside `reconcile_verdict`'s own body (e.g. `plan_review._maybe_apply_completion_floor(result, ...)`) would turn RED. A mutation in `verify_completion`, `deterministic_child_failure`, or the gate_ops path would be invisible to the spy. The AC as written cannot be deterministically verified because 'the call' is undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4: 'The confinement test would fail if `reconcile_verdict` were made to call the floor — verified by a mutation check (temporarily add the call; confirm RED)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact shape of the canned `completion_verdict` dict to pass to `reconcile_verdict` in the confinement test (at minimum: the `verdict` field value and whether `findings` is empty or populated), so an executor can write a non-vacuous test.
findings.finding: The canned `completion_verdict` input shape for the confinement test is never specified in the plan. `reconcile_verdict` operates on the completion_verdict schema (`{verdict, findings}`) — entirely different from the plan-review verdict shape (`{advisory, dropped, coverage}`) that the floor functions consume. The plan does not define what fields the canned input must carry, making the test underspecified: an executor writing the test has no guidance on whether to pass a PASS/FAIL verdict, an empty findings list, or a FAIL with findings.
findings.location: What §2: 'run the deterministic close-path verdict handler `rebar.llm.completion.reconcile_verdict(...)` over a canned `completion_verdict`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mutation for AC4: name the file, the function (`reconcile_verdict`), the exact insertion (e.g. `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict(verdict)` call added at its entry), and the proving command (`make test 2>&1 | grep FAILED`) so the check is reproducible and auditable.
findings.finding: AC4 (mutation check) specifies no concrete command for performing or recording the mutation: 'temporarily add the call; confirm RED' names the action but not the specific edit location, the exact line to add, or the test invocation that produces evidence. An executor cannot mechanically reproduce this check and there is no artifact that records the result.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'The confinement test would fail if reconcile_verdict were made to call the floor — verified by a mutation check (temporarily add the call; confirm RED).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'In `rebar/llm/completion.py`, inside `reconcile_verdict`, temporarily insert `plan_review._apply_completion_floor_to_verdict(verdict)` (or equivalent); run `make test`; confirm the confinement test fails with a spy-call-count assertion error; revert the insertion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe AC4 as a durable outcome: e.g. add a brief comment in the test (or a PR note) that documents the mutation scenario tested, or convert the mutation check into a parametrized pytest mutation fixture so the sensitivity evidence lives in the repo permanently.
findings.finding: AC4 is framed as a one-time manual procedural action ('temporarily add the call; confirm RED') rather than a durable observable end-state. Under F1(c), a criterion should be something that could be false before the work and true (and remain true) because of it — a transient mutate-and-revert action produces no lasting artifact in the repo or CI.

`_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` takes no `repo_root` parameter and is purely in-memory (verified by reading the implementation). The plan's AC step 1 — 'assert NO store mutation escaped' by comparing tickets-branch HEAD sha before/after — will therefore pass VACUOUSLY: the store is structurally unreachable by the function under test, so before==after is guaranteed regardless of what the floor actually does. The plan does not acknowledge this, leaving AC step 3 ('the test fails if a signing/transition/reopen/event-append is introduced') potentially unfalsifiable unless the mutation check is designed to inject a side-effectful call that REACHES the store through some other path.
findings.location: Plan §What, steps 1/4 and AC line: 'Assert NO store mutation escaped: the AFTER store fingerprint is byte-identical to BEFORE'

findings.finding: The plan claims the new test uses `_partial_epic` ('the existing `_partial_epic` gold fixture') inside a real temporary rebar store, but `_partial_epic` is a `monkeypatch`-parameterized helper that replaces all store reads (`rebar._reads.list_tickets`, `rebar._reads.show_ticket`, `attest.delivered_now`) with lambdas. When monkeypatching bypasses the store, `rebar.init_repo` bootstraps a real store that is never queried — the store fingerprint before/after is trivially identical. The plan does not specify how the real store and the monkeypatched reads coexist or which is authoritative, leaving the test design ambiguous for implementors.
findings.location: Plan §What step 1: 'Create a real rebar store in a tmp dir (rebar.init_repo)… then run the floor over the existing _partial_epic gold fixture'

findings.finding: _apply_completion_floor_to_verdict (the function the plan names in AC step 2) is documented as 'Pure (no LLM)' and accepts no repo_root parameter — it has zero I/O pathways to the store. Creating a real rebar store via rebar.init_repo and diffing its HEAD sha / event-file inventory before/after calling this function will trivially always pass (the store is structurally unreachable from this call). The plan's claimed benefit — 'catches ANY signing / transition / reopen / event-append … regardless of how that call is imported' — requires the tested surface to accept repo_root; that is _maybe_apply_completion_floor, not _apply_completion_floor_to_verdict.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace step 2's call target with `plan_review._maybe_apply_completion_floor(ticket_id, verdict, ctx=..., cfg=..., runner=..., repo_root=tmp_repo_root)` (pointing repo_root at the tmp store and setting completion_floor_active=True in the cfg), which has the I/O pathway the mutation guard requires. Use monkeypatch only for the LLM runner call (_classify_completion) so the test stays unit-fast.

findings.finding: The store-fingerprint mutation check is vacuously true for the specific function under test. The plan calls `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` directly — a function documented as 'Pure (no LLM)' with no `repo_root` parameter and no store I/O. Any mutation introduced into this function that touches the store would have to use ambient `REBAR_ROOT`; the plan does not state this dependency explicitly. More critically, the plan's claim that the store fingerprint 'catches ANY signing / transition / reopen / event-append the floor might perform' is only defensible if the test exercises the store-facing outer entry point (`_maybe_apply_completion_floor`, which takes `repo_root` and calls `orchestrator.delivered_children_manifest`). Testing the inner pure function with a real store fingerprint check will always pass trivially, making AC-3 ('The test fails if a signing/transition/reopen/event-append is introduced into the floor') unverifiable as specified.
findings.suggested_fix: In step 2, change the tested function from `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` to `_maybe_apply_completion_floor` (with `completion_floor_active=True` in a monkeypatched config, `repo_root=<tmp_dir>`, and stubbed LLM classifier returning the gold map). This makes the store fingerprint assertion non-vacuous: `_maybe_apply_completion_floor` calls `orchestrator.delivered_children_manifest` which reads from the store via `repo_root`, so any inadvertent signing/transition call in that pipeline would mutate the store and be caught. Alternatively, if the inner pure function is preferred, add an explicit plan note: 'REBAR_ROOT must be set (not just repo_root=) to the tmp store, because `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` has no repo_root parameter — only ambient-env side-effects are caught.'

findings.finding: `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` is documented as 'Pure (no LLM)' and accepts no `repo_root` parameter — it has zero I/O pathways to the store. The plan's AC step 1 and step 4 specify creating a real rebar store and asserting its BEFORE/AFTER fingerprint is unchanged, but calling this pure function cannot ever mutate a store regardless of what store exists. The detection mechanism described is structurally incoherent with the function's verified contract.
findings.location: What section step 1 & step 4; AC step 1 & step 4

findings.finding: AC3 ('The test fails if a signing/transition/reopen/event-append is introduced — verified by a mutation check (temporarily add such a call and confirm RED)') describes a manual developer action with no automated proving command. A CI run cannot produce evidence that this check was actually performed.
findings.suggested_fix: State the exact mutation to apply (e.g., 'add `sign_manifest(...)` inside `_apply_completion_floor_to_verdict` before the return') and the exact command (`make test 2>&1 | grep FAILED`) plus expected output, so a reviewer can reproduce it deterministically.

findings.finding: AC3 ('Test fails if a signing/transition call is introduced') has no mapped step or proving command that demonstrates the RED behavior. An executor who writes passing spies but accidentally writes them incorrectly (e.g., patches the wrong import path) would satisfy AC1+AC4 while AC3 silently fails — the plan provides no command to evidence the RED path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Test fails if a signing/transition call is introduced (even via a helper/rename).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC step such as: 'Adversarial check: temporarily add `sign_manifest(...)` inside the floor function (or a helper it calls), run `pytest tests/unit/test_completion_floor_e2e.py::test_non_regression_floor_is_review_only` and confirm it exits non-zero, then revert.' This can be a manual smoke step or a dedicated xfail sub-test.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
